response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
As an immigrant woman living in Bologna with a lower secondary education, I've seen firsthand how fake content can mess things up online. Growing up Buddhist and being married with two kids makes me value mindfulness and truthfulness. When I use social media just a few times monthly for hobbies like painting or football (calcio), I'm not constantly glued to it, but that doesn't mean I ignore the problem.

I think managing fake content starts with knowing what's real versus fake. My high conscientiousness means I try to be careful and check things out before believing them. For example, when my husband started a Facebook group for local cycling enthusiasts after we moved here from Italy many years ago, we were cautious about who posted photos of our hobby spots.

Also, being an immigrant in Bologna with low openness scores helps me stay grounded; I don't jump into every viral trend without thinking. We're more agreeable and normal psychologically, so we rely on community feedback when something feels off online.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
I start by drawing from my personal experience as a Buddhist immigrant in Italy (Bologna) with two children. My lower secondary education level means I focus on practical strategies rather than technical jargon or complex ideas.

Step-by-step: First, recognize that fake content is often designed to provoke reaction for views and engagement—something I've seen firsthand when my husband posts cycling photos online.
Second, develop habits of checking multiple sources and verifying claims before engaging emotionally. Third, be mindful of how much time you spend on social media (a few times monthly) to avoid burnout from constant exposure.
